texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The Ba form of a silicoaluminophosphate-34 (SAPO-34) zeolite membrane was synthesized and investigated for carbon dioxide (CO2)/nitrogen (N2) separation with low CO2 concentration (as low as 5%) in the feed. The e ff ects of the separation temperature, CO2 concentration in the feed, and pressure di ff erence on the separation performance of the Ba form of a SAPO-34 zeolite membrane were investigated. A CO2 gas permeance of 17.5 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa and a CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 78 were attained for the Ba form of a SAPO-34 zeolite membrane at 100 kPa pressure di ff erence, 303 K, and 5% CO2 concentration in the feed. With low CO 2 concentration (5%) in the CO2/N2 mixture, the Ba form of a SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was e ffi cient in CO2/N2 separation and displayed good stability for the

texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) concentration has drawn researchers ' attention in recent years. CO 2 is among the primary greenhouse gases. The greenhouse gas e ff ect is one of the important environmental issues nowadays in view of its severe implication on global climate change such as global warming. 1 -4 There is an urgent need to explore technologies to reduce or control the CO 2 concentration in the atmosphere. Therefore, worldwide considerable e ff orts are put into restricting the emission of CO2 from large emission sources. CO2 separation from nitrogen (N2), in postcombustion processes, is among the main concerns. Nowadays, there is increasing interest among researchers to search for e ffi cient methods for CO2 separation. 5 -9 Conventionally, technologies including amine absorption and pressure-swing adsorption are utilized for CO 2 separation. 10,11 However, there are disadvantages of these technologies such as the complexity of the system, intensive energy requirement, etc. 12,13

texts.label: text
texts.orig: gained importance for gas separation because of their small pore structure. In view of their small pore structure, which is close to N2 but larger than CO2, they are considered to be potential candidates for CO 2 separation from N2. Most of the CO2 gas separation studies using zeolite membranes were performed for gas streams with 50% CO2 concentration. However, the search for e ffi cient zeolite membranes that are capable of separating CO2 from gas streams with low CO2 concentration still remains one of the research challenges nowadays. It is essential to explore the application of zeolite membranes for CO2 separation from gas streams with low CO 2 concentration because the common CO2 concentration in postcombustion processing stream may go as low as 5%. 5

texts.orig: 2. EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
texts.text: 2. EXPERIMENTAL SECTION

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.1. Materials and Chemicals. Aluminum isopropoxide (98%, Merck), phosphoric acid (85%, Sigma-Aldrich), tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH; 35 wt %), barium acetate salt (>99%, Sigma-Aldrich), deionized water (DI H2O), and colloidal silica sol (Ludox AS-40) were used as chemicals. The membrane support used was an α -alumina disk with a diameter of 2.5 cm.
texts.text: 2.1. Materials and Chemicals. Aluminum isopropoxide (98%, Merck), phosphoric acid (85%, Sigma-Aldrich), tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH; 35 wt %), barium acetate salt (>99%, Sigma-Aldrich), deionized water (DI H2O), and colloidal silica sol (Ludox AS-40) were used as chemicals. The membrane support used was an α -alumina disk with a diameter of 2.5 cm.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.2. Membrane Synthesis. The H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was synthesized by following the procedures reported by our previous study. 57 The synthesis precursor solution with a molar ratio of 1:1:1.2:0.3:80 Al2O3/P2O5/ TEAOH/SiO2/H2O was used to synthesize the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane. In order to prepare the synthesis precursor solution, phosphoric acid, aluminum isopropoxide, and DI H2O were mixed, followed by stirring of the solution for 12 h at room temperature. Then, the solution was mixed with Ludox AS-40 and TEAOH, followed by aging of the resultant mixture at room temperature for 24 h. A Te fl on-lined vessel, which contained an α -alumina disk, was fi lled with the aged synthesis precursor solution. The Te fl on-lined vessel was sealed. MW heating was applied to heat the sealed Te fl on-lined vessel at 473 K for 2 h. Then, the zeolite membrane formed on the α -alumina disk was taken out from the Te fl on-lined vessel and washed with DI H2O, followed by drying at 373 K for 5 h. The same α -alumina disk was again subjected to the same heating procedures three times. The H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane formed on the α -alumina disk was calcined at 673 K for 15 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifuging the synthesis precursor solution from the Te fl on-lined vessel. The collected precipitate was subjected to rinsing with DI H2O and drying in an oven. The dried precipitate was calcined at 673 K for 15 h to obtain the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite particles.
texts.text: 2.2. Membrane Synthesis. The H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was synthesized by following the procedures reported by our previous study. 57 The synthesis precursor solution with a molar ratio of 1:1:1.2:0.3:80 Al2O3/P2O5/ TEAOH/SiO2/H2O was used to synthesize the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane. In order to prepare the synthesis precursor solution, phosphoric acid, aluminum isopropoxide, and DI H2O were mixed, followed by stirring of the solution for 12 h at room temperature. Then, the solution was mixed with Ludox AS-40 and TEAOH, followed by aging of the resultant mixture at room temperature for 24 h. A Te fl on-lined vessel, which contained an α -alumina disk, was fi lled with the aged synthesis precursor solution. The Te fl on-lined vessel was sealed. MW heating was applied to heat the sealed Te fl on-lined vessel at 473 K for 2 h. Then, the zeolite membrane formed on the α -alumina disk was taken out from the Te fl on-lined vessel and washed with DI H2O, followed by drying at 373 K for 5 h. The same α -alumina disk was again subjected to the same heating procedures three times. The H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane formed on the α -alumina disk was calcined at 673 K for 15 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifuging the synthesis precursor solution from the Te fl on-lined vessel. The collected precipitate was subjected to rinsing with DI H2O and drying in an oven. The dried precipitate was calcined at 673 K for 15 h to obtain the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite particles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to prepare the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane, ion exchange of the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane with a Ba 2+ cation was performed by following the procedures reported by our previous study. 57 During the ionexchange process, the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was subjected to re fl ux in a 0.02 M solution containing Ba + for 5 h at 343 K. The ion-exchanged membrane was subjected to rinsing with ethanol, followed by drying in an oven overnight. The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite particles was obtained by following the same ion-exchange procedures for the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane. The zeolite membrane and particles were stored until further use.
texts.text: In order to prepare the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane, ion exchange of the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane with a Ba 2+ cation was performed by following the procedures reported by our previous study. 57 During the ionexchange process, the H form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was subjected to re fl ux in a 0.02 M solution containing Ba + for 5 h at 343 K. The ion-exchanged membrane was subjected to rinsing with ethanol, followed by drying in an oven overnight. The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite particles was obtained by following the same ion-exchange procedures for the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane. The zeolite membrane and particles were stored until further use.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.3. Characterization of the Ba Form of the SAPO-34 Zeolite. The morphology of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was evaluated by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; SUPRA 35VP-24-58) operating at 3 kV. The pore channel of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite was observed by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; model TECNAI 20).

The morphology of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was evaluated by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; SUPRA 35VP-24-58) operating at 3 kV. The pore channel of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite was observed by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; model TECNAI 20).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.4. Gas Separation Studies. Prior to the gas separation studies, a membrane module that contained the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was located in an oven. CO2 and N2 gases were fed to the membrane module in the oven, in order to obtain CO2/N2 gas mixtures. The CO2 concentration in the feed was varied from 5 to 50%. All of the gas separation studies were carried out by maintaining the membrane permeate pressure at atmospheric pressure. The pressure of the membrane feed side was regulated in order to vary the pressure di ff erence across the Ba form of the SAPO-34 membrane from 100 to 500 kPa. The temperature of the oven was varied in order to study the gas separation performance of the membrane at di ff erent separation temperatures from 303 to 453 K. Gas chromatography (Perkin Elmer CLARUS 500) was used to analyze the composition of the permeate and retentate streams. The fl ow rates of the permeate and retentate streams were measured.
texts.text: 2.4. Gas Separation Studies. Prior to the gas separation studies, a membrane module that contained the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was located in an oven. CO2 and N2 gases were fed to the membrane module in the oven, in order to obtain CO2/N2 gas mixtures. The CO2 concentration in the feed was varied from 5 to 50%. All of the gas separation studies were carried out by maintaining the membrane permeate pressure at atmospheric pressure. The pressure of the membrane feed side was regulated in order to vary the pressure di ff erence across the Ba form of the SAPO-34 membrane from 100 to 500 kPa. The temperature of the oven was varied in order to study the gas separation performance of the membrane at di ff erent separation temperatures from 303 to 453 K. Gas chromatography (Perkin Elmer CLARUS 500) was used to analyze the composition of the permeate and retentate streams. The fl ow rates of the permeate and retentate streams were measured.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.1. Characterization of the Ba Form of the SAPO-34 Zeolite. Figure 1 shows the top-view SEM image of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane, and Figure 2 shows the cross-sectional-view SEM image of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane. It is observed from Figure 1 that a Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane, which consists of orthorhombic zeolite crystals of about 1 μ m size, was formed in the current study. On the other hand, a Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane with a thickness of about 4 μ m is observed in Figure 2. Figure 3 shows the HRTEM image of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite. The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite crystals was formed with a uniform pore channel of less than 0.5 nm diameter.

texts.orig: membrane in the CO2/N2 binary mixture with di ff erent CO 2 concentrations in the feed at 100 kPa pressure di ff erence and 303 K. The CO2 and N2 gas fl uxes diplayed di ff erent trends of change when the CO2 concentration in the feed was increased from 5 to 50%. The CO2 gas fl ux increased almost linearly with an increase in the CO2 concentration in the feed. This is because of the increase in the CO 2 adsorption coverage at the feed side of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane when the CO2 concentration in the feed was increased from 5 to 50%. On the other hand, the N2 gas fl ux displayed a maximum point when the CO2 concentration in the feed was varied. A slight increase in the N 2 gas fl ux [(2.2 -2.6) × 10 -3 mol/m 2 · s] was fi rst observed when the CO2 concentration in the feed was increased from 5 to 14%. This is because N2 permeation through the membrane pore was slightly boosted by an increase in the CO 2 adsorption coverage of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane when the CO2 concentration in the feed was increased in the low CO 2 concentration range. However, further increasing the CO2 concentration in the feed from 14 to 50% resulted in a decrease in the N 2 gas fl ux. This phenomenon might be due to the presence of a higher concentration of more adsorbable CO2 molecules in the feed, which posted a hindrance e ff ect on the N2 permeation through the membrane pore for a further increase in the CO2 concentration in the feed from 14 to 50%. 58,59 Hence, a maximum N2 gas fl ux at 14% CO2 concentration in the feed resulted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4. Comparison for the Gas Separation Performance of the Other Zeolite Membranes with the Ba Form of the SAPO-34 Zeolite Membrane. The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was compared with the other reported zeolite membranes for its CO2/N2 gas separation performance. As shown in Table 1, a number of research works had been reported for CO2/N2 separation using a MFI zeolite membrane. Shin et al. 21 obtained a low CO2 gas permeance of 0.36 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa for a 50% CO2/50% N2 gas mixture separation at 298 K by using the MFI zeolite membrane. In the other work by Singh et al., 62 a very low CO2 gas permeance of ∼ 0.03 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa was reported for the β -zeolite membrane in separating a gas mixture with 50% CO2/50% N2 at 298 K. The reported CO2/N2 separation selectivity or separation factor dropped when the temperature was increased, and the same phenomenon was found in the present study. Li and Fan 10 synthesized the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane with a CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 32 and a CO 2 gas permeance of 12 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa for a 50% CO2/50% N2 gas mixture at 295 K. In recent work by Jang et al., 60 the highest CO2/N2 separation factor of only ∼ 12.5 was obtained for the gas separation involving the 50% CO2/50% N2 gas mixture through CHA-type zeolite membranes. In the present study, a high CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 78 was achieved for separating the CO2/N2 gas mixture with low CO2 concentration (5%) at 303 K by using the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane.

The reported CO2/N2 separation selectivity or separation factor dropped when the temperature was increased, and the same phenomenon was found in the present study. Li and Fan 10 synthesized the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane with a CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 32 and a CO 2 gas permeance of 12 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa for a 50% CO2/50% N2 gas mixture at 295 K. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: 4. CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was successfully formed, and it consists of orthorhombic zeolite crystals with about 1 μ m size and a uniform pore channel of <0.5 nm diameter. The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane in the present study gave satisfactory-to-high separation performance of CO2/N2 for a wide range of CO2 concentrations (5 -50%) in the feed studied. The highest CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 78 and CO 2 gas permeance of 17.5 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa were attained for the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane at 100 kPa pressure di ff erence, 303 K, and 5% CO2 concentration in the feed. The CO2 gas permeance decreased with an increase in the separation temperature from 303 to 453 K due to the reduced CO2 adsorption coverage of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane and, hence, resulted in a drop in the CO2/N2 separation selectivity. On the other hand, a decrease in the CO2/N2 separation selectivity of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane occurred when the pressure di ff erence was increased from 100 to 500 kPa. In the current study, the formed Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was e ffi cient in separating CO2 from N2, with a low CO2 concentration in the gas mixture. The stability of the zeolite membrane ' s separation performance for CO2/N2 gas mixtures with low CO2 concentration (5%) in the feed was also con fi rmed via the durability test in the current study.

The Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane in the present study gave satisfactory-to-high separation performance of CO2/N2 for a wide range of CO2 concentrations (5 -50%) in the feed studied. The highest CO2/N2 separation selectivity of 78 and CO 2 gas permeance of 17.5 × 10 -7 mol/m 2 · s · Pa were attained for the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane at 100 kPa pressure di ff erence, 303 K, and 5% CO2 concentration in the feed. The CO2 gas permeance decreased with an increase in the separation temperature from 303 to 453 K due to the reduced CO2 adsorption coverage of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane and, hence, resulted in a drop in the CO2/N2 separation selectivity. On the other hand, a decrease in the CO2/N2 separation selectivity of the Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane occurred when the pressure di ff erence was increased from 100 to 500 kPa. In the current study, the formed Ba form of the SAPO-34 zeolite membrane was e ffi cient in separating CO2 from N2, with a low CO2 concentration in the gas mixture. The stability of the zeolite membrane ' s separation performance for CO2/N2 gas mixtures with low CO2 concentration (5%) in the feed was also con fi rmed via the durability test in the current study.
